facts_disclosed: Flying to Tokyo, departing March 20 2026, for a conference

turns.content: OKAY i just booked my tokyo flights for the conference in march!! march 20th departure, so excited i can barely focus on work rn
Tokyo!! That's so exciting — how long are you going for? And is this your first time there or have you been before?
first time ever!! i'm going for about 10 days, conference is the first few days and then i have like 6 days to just... be in japan. i've wanted to go for literally years

Ten days with six free days after the conference is a really good amount of time — enough to actually settle in and not feel like you're sprinting through everything. Late March is also an excellent time to be there: cherry blossom season typically peaks around late March to early April in Tokyo, so you might catch it. Are you planning to stay in Tokyo the whole time or thinking about day trips or other cities?

i was thinking mostly tokyo but maybe a day trip to kyoto or somewhere? i've heard the shinkansen is amazing and i feel like i have to try it at least once. also cherry blossoms omg i didn't even think about that, that would be incredible

The shinkansen is genuinely one of those experiences that lives up to the hype — Tokyo to Kyoto is about 2 hours and 15 minutes and it's absurdly smooth and punctual. For a day trip though, Kyoto is a lot to try to cram into one day, especially if you want to actually soak it in rather than just say you went. A lot of people find that Kamakura or Nikko work better as day trips from Tokyo since they're only about an hour away, and both are stunning in different ways. Kamakura has the giant Buddha and beautiful coastal temples. Nikko has this insane ornate shrine complex in cedar forest.

If you want to do Kyoto properly, it might be worth spending 2 nights there and building it into the trip rather than rushing it as a day trip. Kyoto in late March with cherry blossoms at Maruyama Park or the Philosopher's Path would be genuinely unforgettable. What's your accommodation situation looking like — are you staying near the conference venue the whole time?
i'm in a hotel near the conference for the first part, then i was going to move somewhere else after. hadn't figured that out yet. maybe i do 2 nights in kyoto?? now i'm redesigning my whole trip lol

Two nights in Kyoto is a great call — that gives you full days on both sides plus travel time. Here's a rough shape that could work: conference days in your hotel, then after it wraps take the shinkansen to Kyoto, spend two nights there, come back to Tokyo for your remaining days. For the Kyoto stretch you'd want to be in the Gion or Higashiyama area to be walking distance from most of the good stuff.

For Tokyo accommodation after the conference, neighborhoods like Shinjuku, Shibuya, and Shimokitazawa all have very different vibes. Shinjuku is the full sensory overload experience, Shibuya is slightly hipper and more walkable, Shimokitazawa is this indie/vintage neighborhood that's quieter and feels very local. What's your general travel style — do you want to be in the middle of everything or do you like a slightly quieter base you can escape to?

i think middle of everything, i get fomo if i feel like i'm missing stuff. also i need to ask — i'm pescatarian, is that going to be really hard in japan? i've heard the food is amazing but also i feel like everything has dashi in it

Okay so the dashi thing is real — it's in a LOT of things, including broths that look vegetarian but aren't technically. If you're strictly avoiding fish stock specifically, that's genuinely harder in Japan. But if pescatarian for you means fish is fine (just no meat), then you're actually in an incredible situation. Japan is one of the best countries in the world if you eat seafood.

Sushi and sashimi are obvious, but honestly the whole range of things opens up: ramen shops that specialize in seafood broth (shio ramen is often made with clam or fish dashi and it's stunning), tempura restaurants where you can do a full tempura meal of shrimp, squid, and vegetables, soba, chirashi bowls, kaiseki courses built around seafood. For pure fish-forward eating, Tsukiji Outer Market in the morning is a must — it's where you eat fresh tuna sashimi and tamagoyaki at 8am and feel like you've unlocked a cheat code.

Is there any food stuff you're especially excited about or things you're nervous about navigating?

omg i NEED to do tsukiji. and i'm really excited about ramen, i've been wanting to try authentic ramen for so long. i'm also slightly nervous about navigating things generally since i don't speak japanese at all

Ramen in Japan is going to ruin all other ramen for you forever, just so you're prepared. Tokyo alone has multiple distinct regional styles — you can literally spend a week eating a different bowl every day and they're all distinct.

For navigation and language: honestly Tokyo is very foreigner-friendly in 2026. Most train stations have English signage, Google Maps works extremely well there for transit, and a lot of restaurants have picture menus or plastic food displays outside. The Google Translate camera function is a lifesaver for menus that are only in Japanese — just point your camera at it and it overlays translations in real time. That one tool removes about 90% of the anxiety.

A few practical things worth doing before you go: get a Suica card (IC card) set up on your phone for transit — it works on basically every train and bus in Tokyo and Kyoto and you can load it with Apple Pay or Google Pay. Get a pocket WiFi or data SIM sorted in advance. And download offline Google Maps for the Tokyo and Kyoto regions before you leave just in case.

Also — are you going alone or is anyone coming with you?
going alone! raj can't take the time off work. honestly i'm kind of excited about solo travel, it's been a while since i did a solo trip

Tokyo solo is honestly ideal — it's one of the safest cities in the world, extremely easy to navigate alone, and the solo-traveler culture is just built in. Restaurants that are just a counter with individual seats (counter sushi, ramen bars with little partition booths) are designed for solo dining in a way that feels celebratory rather than lonely. You can completely follow your own whims, which matters when you're also there for a conference and need to manage your energy.

Is there anything specific you want to make sure you don't miss — like a neighborhood, a type of experience, something you've seen and always wanted to do? March is also perfect timing because it's not peak summer heat, not rainy season, and if the blossoms are out it'll be spectacular.

i really want to see akihabara even though i'm not like a hardcore anime person, it just looks so surreal and interesting. and i want to walk through a huge covered shopping street. and honestly just... wander? i think the wandering is the thing

Akihabara is absolutely worth it even as a non-anime person — the density of it is kind of remarkable, it's just visually overwhelming in the best way. Go during the day and also at night because the neon version is completely different. And the covered shopping arcade you're thinking of is probably Nakamise in Asakusa (very touristy but genuinely fun) or the shotengai in Yanaka, which is this old-school neighborhood that somehow survived the war with most of its traditional shops intact. Yanaka feels like a different era of Tokyo and it's great for exactly the kind of wandering you're describing.

Shimokitazawa is also perfect for wandering — narrow lanes, vintage clothing shops, tiny jazz bars, coffee places. You find stuff by accident there.

You've got a great trip ahead. March 20th will be here before you know it.
i'm literally so excited i'm going to combust. ok i need to actually get back to work but i'm going to spend the next week just daydreaming about this trip

Totally earned. You've got three months to slowly build the anticipation — look up the ramen shops, save the Yanaka neighborhood walk, check the cherry blossom forecast as it gets closer. It's going to be a great trip.
